claims.text: Mount Rushmore conceals a secret chamber that is unknown to most visitors.

evidence.quote: mount rushmore has a secret

claims.rationale: The Hall of Records does exist and is not open to public tours, but it is not classified or 'secret' in any espionage sense — the National Park Service acknowledges it openly. The episode uses 'secret' in a promotional sense.

claims.text: Mount Rushmore was sculpted between 1927 and 1941 under architect Gutzon Borglum.

evidence.quote:
was constructed between 1927 and 1941
under the direction of architect gutson
borglum

claims.rationale: Construction dates (Oct 4, 1927 – Oct 31, 1941) and Gutzon Borglum's role are basic, well-sourced historical fact.

claims.text: Mount Rushmore sits on land that was stolen from Native American (Lakota) tribes, undermining its framing as a monument to justice.

evidence.quote:
justice tell that to the indians they
stole the land from

claims.rationale: Historical consensus: the Black Hills were guaranteed to the Lakota by the 1868 Fort Laramie Treaty and seized after the 1874 Custer expedition. United States v. Sioux Nation of Indians (1980) upheld this as a taking without just compensation.

claims.text: In 1924, South Dakota historian Doane Robinson proposed carving a monument inspired by the Stone Mountain Confederate memorial in Georgia.

evidence.quote:
in 1924 he learned about an attempt to
carve the likenesses of confederate
leaders into the side of stone mountain
in georgia

claims.rationale: Doane Robinson (the transcript's 'dawn robinson' is an ASR typo) is the historically documented originator of the Mount Rushmore idea in 1923–1924, inspired by Stone Mountain.

claims.text: The Stone Mountain Memorial Association fired Borglum in February 1925 for mismanagement of funds and 'offensive egotism'.

evidence.quote:
in february 1925 the association
fired borglum

citing mismanagement of funds and his
offensive egotism and his delusions of
grandeur

claims.rationale: Borglum's February 1925 firing from Stone Mountain is well-documented. The 'offensive egotism' wording is period-accurate, from the Association's own statement.

claims.text: After being fired, Borglum destroyed his Stone Mountain models and fled Georgia to avoid prosecution.

evidence.quote:
borglum made national news when he
destroyed the stone mountain models
and fled the state

claims.rationale: Borglum did destroy his plaster models (to prevent the Association from completing the work without him) and fled to North Carolina to evade a warrant. Matter of newspaper record.

claims.text: Borglum envisioned a grand Hall of Records, 80 ft tall by 100 ft long, accessed by an 800 ft granite staircase, holding founding documents behind Abraham Lincoln's hairline.

evidence.quote:
he envisioned a grand
hall measuring 80 feet tall and 100 feet
long

the vault was built to be just behind
abraham lincoln's hairline

claims.rationale: Borglum's grand Hall of Records proposal (including the 800-foot granite stairway and the location behind Lincoln's hairline) is documented in his correspondence and NPS archives.

claims.text: Construction of the Hall of Records began in July 1938 but Congress halted it a year later.

evidence.quote:
work on the hall of
records began in july 1938 but was
halted a year later

claims.rationale: NPS records confirm Hall of Records work started July 1938 and Congress stopped funding in July 1939.

claims.text: Gutzon Borglum died on March 6, 1941, and his son Lincoln took over the project.

evidence.quote:
he died on march 6 1941 leaving his son
lincoln to continue the work

claims.rationale: Gutzon Borglum's March 6, 1941 death and his son Lincoln Borglum's succession to the project are verified historical facts.

claims.text: Jefferson was originally meant to be on Washington's right side but the stone there was too weak, forcing improvisation of his placement.

evidence.quote:
jefferson was originally
supposed to be on washington's right
side but the stone was too weak

claims.rationale: Documented: the first Jefferson carving was abandoned after 18 months due to poor rock quality and relocated to the left of Washington.

claims.text: The project was declared complete on October 31, 1941, but the statues were never finished as Borglum planned.

evidence.quote:
the project was declared
final and complete on october 31st
1941

the statues are incomplete the
inscription was never started

claims.rationale: Oct 31, 1941 termination and incompleteness (no carving down to waists, no inscription) are documented by the NPS.

claims.text: In 1998 a scaled-back Hall of Records was installed — porcelain enamel panels of the Constitution, Bill of Rights, Declaration and Gettysburg Address sealed in a titanium vault behind a 1,200-pound granite slab.

evidence.quote:
in 1998
officials agreed to a scaled-back
version of the idea

behind a giant
1200 pound granite slab

claims.rationale: The Aug 9, 1998 installation of the teakwood chest with 16 porcelain enamel panels behind a titanium capstone and 1,200-lb granite slab is NPS documented fact.

claims.text: The Hall of Records is closed to the public, and even high-ranking government officials are banned from seeing the chamber.

evidence.quote:
records
is not open to the public

even high
ranking officials in the government are
banned from seeing this hidden
and largely unknown chamber

claims.rationale: The 'closed to the public' part is fully true (NPS access restricted to maintenance). The 'high-ranking officials banned' phrasing is stronger than the sourcing supports — there is no published directive explicitly banning officials; it's more accurate to say access is limited to a few park personnel.

claims.text: Greenpeace breached Mount Rushmore's security in 2009 by hanging a 'Stop Global Warming' banner over the monument.

evidence.quote:
following a breach by greenpeace who
hung a banner
reading stop global warming over the
monument in 2009.

claims.rationale: Verified: July 8, 2009 Greenpeace hung 'America Honors Leaders, Not Politicians: Stop Global Warming' banner over Mount Rushmore; 12 activists were arrested.

claims.text: Borglum believed Mount Rushmore might someday be as mysterious to future civilizations as Stonehenge or the Egyptian pyramids, motivating the Hall of Records as a time capsule.

evidence.quote:
the vault was built to be just behind
abraham lincoln's hairline

claims.rationale: Borglum explicitly wrote about Mount Rushmore as a monument for future civilizations comparable to ancient wonders; the motivation is real. The speculative side is whether future archaeologists would actually see it that way.
